Period. There's a new weight loss infomercial, broke not too long ago. I watched half of it this morning. It's a redo of the old deal-a-meal. Anybody remember the deal-a-meal with Richard Simmons? You move the red card to the blue slot, da-da-da-da. Well, you should pay attention to this show. It's very well done. It's going to make a fortune. And they built a fairly complicated product. So they got this, like, book with little tabs and stuff cut, and you fill out a questionnaire, and you figure out your body type, and then when you turn it over on the back, after you've put the right pieces together, it tells you what to eat. But the best thing they got in the whole pitch, here's the bonus. It's a pocket sized book for 50 of the major restaurant chains in America, and it's got their logos all over it. Bob Evans, Applebee's, McDonald's, etc. One page for each one of them that tells you what to eat to lose weight while you're there. So here's the foods to eat at Bob Evans to crank up your metabolism, and it's in a book. You walk in, you open the book, you look at the menu, it told you what to eat. I'm looking at this and I'm saying, the phones going to ring off the friggin hook right now, because here's what they did that's so smart. First of all, everybody else is telling everybody, stay the hell out of them places. All right? If you want to lose weight, the first thing you do is don't go there. Get your nose out of the trough and you'll be better off, right? But see, that's a profit nobody wants. The profit who shows up and says, by all means, eat lunch at McDonald's, go to Outback for dinner, and stop by Krispy Kreme in the morning on the way to work. And here's a page for each one of them that tells you what to eat that will crank up your metabolism. This guy is the most welcome profit yet to arrive this year for weight loss. It's brilliant because it's this. It's exactly what to do, no thought required, and it's actually what they want to do. Clever. I don't know who's show it is, but boy, it's going to crank money. So the general desire, back to general and specific, right? The general desire is enunciated up here. Millions of people, they want to pray, but they don't know what to say, so we're going to give them prayers. The specific desire is down here lower, okay? So they were the same subjects they had seen in the letters from the buyers of the rabbit's feet. So people wanted to pray about money, love, health, etc. So they had identified their customers' needs. So now they could write prayers for each thing that people wanted. They don't want to believe it doesn't work. Cool. It also tells you it goes on so they decided to get a conscience. So they wrote a series of mimeographed lessons on practical subjects to tell the people how to do things for themselves, like how to get out of debt, how to get a job. Sold 20 prayers for every one of the lessons. People want miracles, not how to do information. Well, see, like in the psychic business, they attribute everything that goes right or astrology. The believer attributes everything that goes right to the horoscope, attributes everything that goes wrong to them. That's correct. That's correct. Yeah. So page 187, probably the last exercise before launch, I think. Two things. What do people believe in general that you can show up and agree with in order to influence them? And what do the people you seek to influence believe specifically that you can show up and agree with them about? So we're looking for how you get to be a welcomed prophet rather than an unwelcomed prophet.
